mappings.rationaleAI: Pheromones function as chemical communication signals governing alarm and predator avoidance responses (A.3), sexual attractiveness and reproductive behaviour (A.6), and intraspecific communication that organizes social insect colonies including caste determination in honey bees (C.2, C.3, C.4).

Behavioural ecology directly studies animal decision-making about feeding (A.1), territorial defence and aggression (A.4), habitat use (A.5), mating and reproduction (A.6), and social cooperation (C.3, C.4), integrating evolutionary biology with field observation of behavioural diversity across species.

Complexity science examines how organisms behave in groups in emergent, unpredictable ways that differ from individual behaviour, with examples such as bird flocking illustrating self-organisation and nonlinear dynamics in collective animal behaviour (C.4).

Cephalopods demonstrate sophisticated avoidance behaviours including camouflage, colour change, and ink squirting (A.3), alongside remarkable problem-solving abilities and intelligence in invertebrates that illustrate complex cognitive capacities and reasoning beyond vertebrates (B.5, B.5.c).

The evolutionary paradox of altruistic behaviour — costly kindness explained through Dawkins's gene-centred perspective — addresses how natural selection shapes cooperative and self-sacrificing social behaviour among animals, central to the range and dynamics of social behaviour (C.3, C.4).

Slime moulds exhibit collective problem-solving without nervous systems, demonstrating emergent group behaviour and decision-making capacities (B.5) in organisms that challenge conventional boundaries between social and nonsocial groups (C.3, C.4).

Pollinator behaviour — insects, birds, and mammals responding to floral coloration, scent, and morphology — illustrates food-getting behaviour (A.1) shaped by co-evolutionary dynamics, while plant reproductive adaptations drive pollinator behavioural patterns related to reproduction (A.6).
